summary: Terrie E. Moffitt discusses her longitudinal research with the Dunedin study, focusing on the 'pace of aging.' She explains how biological aging is a coordinated decline of organ systems distinct from acute disease, and highlights how early-life adversity, mental health disorders, and personality traits like conscientiousness strongly predict how fast a person ages. She advocates for incorporating biological age into social policies and medical decision-making.
themes: Mental health impacts on physical aging

principles.statement: Aging is not a disease; it is the coordinated decline of organ systems over time.
principles.rationale: It is important to separate the gradual, coordinated deterioration of the body's systems over decades from temporary periods of acute illness or disease spikes.
principles.source_quote: it was very wrong-headed to think about aging as a disease... what my team is really trying to do is to separate the measurement of aging from just being sick

principles.statement: Mental disorders are early predictors of accelerated physical aging.
principles.rationale: Mental health issues typically onset in a person's teens or 20s, while age-related physical diseases appear much later. A history of mental disorders strongly correlates with a faster pace of biological aging in midlife.
principles.source_quote: people who have mental disorders and the more mental disorders they have for the longer number of years those things all are strong predictors of their pace of Aging when they reach midlife

principles.rationale: People who are careful, planful, and responsible with their finances tend to apply that same level of care to their health behaviors, resulting in slower biological aging.
principles.source_quote: people who are not conscientious about their money are not conscientious about their health

principles.statement: Chronological age is an insufficient metric for retirement and medical policy.
principles.rationale: There is massive individual variation in biological aging. Raising the retirement age based solely on chronological age ignores that some individuals are biologically much older and physically incapable of continuing to work.
principles.source_quote: there are many people who are let's say eligible for Social Security at at age 62 their biological age may be age 70 so they have difficult making it they can't keep working their bodies are falling apart

frameworks.name: Pace of Aging Measurement
frameworks.when_to_apply: When determining how fast an individual's biological systems are declining compared to their chronological age.
steps: Collect biomarkers from multiple bodily systems (e.g., blood chemistry, dental, respiratory) repeatedly over many years.
Model the longitudinal data to identify the gradual, coordinated rate of decline across all organ systems, filtering out temporary disease spikes.
Correlate this longitudinal decline with epigenetic marks on the DNA to create a single score.
Export this score so it can be tested in individuals via a simple blood test to determine their biological pace of aging.
frameworks.source_quote: we track all of the different organ systems as the people have been growing up... we take all those data and we model them so that we come up with an indicator a single score for each individual of how fast their organs have been declining together

mental_models.name: The Brain as the Canary in the Coal Mine
mental_models.description: A lens for understanding the link between mental and physical health, suggesting that the brain is highly sensitive and shows signs of malfunction (via mental health disorders) before the rest of the body's organ systems begin to visibly decline.
mental_models.example: Explaining why early-life mental disorders like depression or schizophrenia predict faster physical aging and earlier death decades later.

heuristics: People who are not conscientious about their money are not conscientious about their health.
To measure aging accurately, look for the coordinated decline of all organ systems over time, not just isolated biomarker spikes.

signature_quotes.text: it was very wrong-headed to think about aging as a disease... what my team is really trying to do is to separate the measurement of aging from just being sick
signature_quotes.context: Explaining her fundamental approach to studying aging.

signature_quotes.text: people who are not conscientious about their money are not conscientious about their health
signature_quotes.context: Discussing the link between personality traits, credit ratings, and biological aging.

signature_quotes.text: the vast majority of people who are ever going to have a mental disorder in their life will have it in their teens or in their 20s... and then the age related diseases such as heart disease diabetes Alzheimer's come late
signature_quotes.context: Explaining the temporal relationship between mental health and physical aging.

signature_quotes.text: there are many people who are let's say eligible for Social Security at at age 62 their biological age may be age 70 so they have difficult making it they can't keep working their bodies are falling apart
signature_quotes.context: Arguing against raising the retirement age based purely on chronological age.

anti_patterns.description: Equating chronological age with biological capacity for policy or medical decisions.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It ignores massive individual variation. Using strict chronological cutoffs for retirement or surgery eligibility penalizes those who age biologically faster due to genetics, early adversity, or lifestyle.

anti_patterns.description: Measuring aging by looking at a single point-in-time biomarker spike.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: A single bad biomarker (like poor lung function at age 32) might just indicate a temporary infection. True aging is the gradual, coordinated decline of multiple organ systems over decades.
